speaker: interviewer
text: --Goldman. We're in Sutton, Surrey, England. And this is tape six. We were just sort of nearly at the end. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What do I want to ask you? How did your-- how did you learn what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, you know, after coming back to-- to the borderline-- because I tried-- wanted to go back to Sadowa Wisznia. And, as it was Russian, I didn't. I met in-- in Przemysl, which is a town next to it, some of the survivors from my town who were in hiding in Sadowa Wisznia. Well, so-- and then you started learning about the stories, about the trains, about Belzec, about-- about Janowska and all the things which-- which were fact. They were exhibition. When I was in Lublin, there was-- not far from Lublin is Majdanek. I went to visit that. And there were some-- I still remember sort of artistic impression or interpretation of the people in the Zyklon in the last minute, their faces. And they are with me all the time. And you saw pictures-- because Belzec is not so well-known. One talks about Auschwitz, Majdanek, Treblinka. Belzec was one of the worst. Belzec was not even a concentration camp. It was just extermination. And at that time, in October, they had one train after another. So some of these people were straight away undressed and had to queue, undressed in this cold weather, waiting for that to come. So these images, you know, whether artistic painting or images describing this period, have left me with all the images I need for that period. I can imagine what's happened. I hope that my grandmother died-- because she had a weak heart-- I hope she died on the train. That when it came, that she was dead by that time. My father died in Janowska. This was also one of the concentration camps. The rest of the family went-- nobody survived-- my aunt, my uncles, my cousins. As I told you, I came from a large family. They were-- all the time I was searching, while I was in Poland, travelling, looking at documents, looking in Yad Vashem, looking everywhere. And the only people I know were the two cousins of my mother was [? Pinek, ?] who lived in Paris. He just died last year. A wonderful person. Survived Auschwitz with a beautiful heart and-- a wonderful human being. A remarkable man. And my cousin in Israel was killed on a road accident in Israel. So-- but there are some people. What-- I go every year to Israel, and I meet some people from my hometown and we talk. As you-- I'll show you some of the drawings where I show the town, because I don't remember names. But if they tell me where the house where they lived, it reminds me. My memory is very vague. It sort of goes. And so I meet some of the people who-- who survived. There were one or two survivors. And some people who came to Israel before the war-- 1936, 1935-- and settled in Israel. And they are like my family. They come to stay with me, and I come with them. So there is this continuity in that respect that they are people from my hometown. And there is nobody else. Nobody-- there is the family-- you see, I was much closer to my mother's family, because we lived together. My father's family lived near Sanok. I mentioned to you that I made contact with them. And they survived in-- in-- in Siberia. And after the war, they came back to Poland. And they-- some of them went to Israel. Some of them in America. And I'm in touch with them. They're cousins, and they live in America. So I visit them from time to time. But otherwise, you know, it's very difficult to accept the fact that, you know, people question the statistics, and it never happened before, and it's a Jewish problem, it's Jewish exaggeration. And there you have-- you know, the nearest. My father was one of seven or eight. My mother was eight. All these had families. Nobody survived. So this is the fact-- these are the facts. You can't get away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found out through-- then later on, as I say, I started reading a lot about this period and learning from what happened to the other cities-- how other-- our town was made Judenfrei. It means any Jews from there was excluded. Part of it was straight away taken to Belzec. Some of it in Jaworow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jaworow, I found out later on, was, in January, burned down. There was typhus there, and they killed the people outside Jaworow. And the rest was burned down. So this was finished in Jaworow. I've never been to Poland back. I don't know. Part of me says, yes, I would like to see the river, the town, the house where I was born. It's still there. Part of me is afraid to go back to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I went back to Berlin to see the 73 Wallenbergstrasse last year. Or was it was two years ago? I knew the people won't be there, but I just wanted to see the house, and I wanted to take the photograph. So I-- especially as this is-- was east Europe, and now it's united. So I went to visit it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I'll have courage after this, next year, to go to Poland. It's-- it's it's difficult, especially the Ukraine. You know, I have to go-- and it's quite dangerous. [INAUDIBLE] of this little town, when they see people who come back, and they're not very pleased to see the-- I'm not going to claim anything. I don't want anything. But it's not the danger. It's just the nightmares to go back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I tried to trace the family, you know, there's-- and I couldn't trace them at all. The woman, particularly, who walked with me and who took me. I've never been able to-- to say thank you to her and to reward. So I always try. If I do something in other direction, I think, this is in her name, you know? Do you know-- do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. I only know that her husband was a postman. And all the other people from my town, I tried to recover. Nobody remembers her name, either. So unless I go to Sadowa Wisznia, look up the documents, then I could find her. She probably is not alive anymore. She would be-- but her daughter, Sasza, being my age, can be alive. But it's-- it's-- I've forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, originally-- you will say, why didn't you look originally? Earlier? My life was here. It was so difficult. I had so many other problems that this wasn't my priority.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's only now, when I have more time and I'm more-- and I can afford doing different searches then I would like to do it. But then the memory is going. And this is why timing is very important. At a certain stage, you can do something. You know, whenever I fundraise for WIZO or something, I always tell the story about my husband's escape, because it was interesting escape. And I say, there was a time you could help. But there was a time no money could help anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a problem, that there is a time you can do something. There is a time that it's too late or you can't do anything about it. Before we finish, is there anything else that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to show the grand-- my photographs. Would you like to see that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we will stop the film and then show--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then show that.

speaker: interviewer
text: --those in a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope that we covered the period. You know, a lot of people say, oh, but you haven't been in the camp, so therefore your experiences are not so horrific. It is true. It is true. I haven't been in a camp. And after being in Lublin-- I used to go to hospital to visit some of the survivors. And I know what it is. But you cannot put a degree of experience. You cannot measure it. You cannot put it on a scale. And all of us who survived are damaged in one form or another. We try to pretend that we are normal. Some do it better, some not. But we all carry the ghosts of the past with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Goldman, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for being so sympathetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe because you were too sympathetic that I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was so moved by what you were saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she has--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got very few photographs from my past, because I didn't-- they didn't survive. But this was given to me by the family that survived in Siberia. And this is my father. He must have been, at that age-- he was getting married. I think he must have been about 24 years old. You can see he's an Orthodox. He's got side payot. He's wearing a cap. He was a very religious Orthodox young man. Strong. And a very, very dedicated man to his studies in Talmud. A good father, a family provider. And a very caring person. This is my mother. Her name was Sarah. And this was probably also about when she was 22, 23. And she's wearing a sheitel. She-- it must have been done for the wedding. Again, a photograph which was given to me after the war. You can see how gentle she is. She was very gentle, caring, loving, and very sensitive person who had a very tough life. When I last met her-- 1942, when we parted-- she looked old. She was a very aged with the experiences. This was the time when she was married. This is my uncle, the oldest brother of my mother, with his wife and the two children. The third person, I don't even know who it is. Again, in one of my visit in Israel, somebody found this photograph, a friend of the family. And I keep it because it's another relic from the past. I don't have very many, and therefore this is very precious. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. And he lived in Lesko, a small town. He was the oldest brother of the family. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is here, no? There should be a date. June-- 6th of June, 1943. Yeah. Yeah, it's there, too, isn't it? 2nd of April.

speaker: interviewee
text: April. Oh, so it's-- this is a different. No, it says 'April.' April, 1943. It's more likely. I don't know about the June [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how this survived. [LAUGHS] I've got nothing from that period, but this I found quite accidentally. And you can see it's joined together. It's a document from the time that I was in Berlin. And you've got the name of the family-- Tea Lang. And the address is Berlin Hessenwinkel, Fahlenbergstrasse 73. It was a document-- she was entitled, as having a domestic, to some clothing, because everything was rationed. And this was outside the ration, the normal procedure. And my name is in it-- arbeiten Olga Demczyzszak. And she was entitled some of the clothes mentioned that. When I look back now, I realize that this pile of clothes that I went in to select must have been clothes which were sent back, cleaned, for distribution, from-- from people from the camps. I didn't know it at the time. I just thought it's a-- it's a-- it's a distribution center. But when I look back at it, I realize what it was. But it's one of the document that I myself sometimes need a confirmation that I was Olga Demczyzszak, that I was in Berlin in 1943. This is the group which we-- when we arrived to England in 1946. We were in Stoke Newington, and I don't even remember the address, but it was a children's home. And the lady in the middle was a nurse, and a wonderful person who worked with Rabbi Schonfeld. It was-- we were the first Rabbi Schonfeld group. And I don't have enough time or even-- to emphasize the importance Rabbi Schonfeld played in my life. He was a man who gave me a new lease of life. He came from outside, and he gave me a helping hand. He was really my-- my-- my survivor-- my savior. I'm on this picture on the second row, second from the right. One or two of the friends from this group, I am in touch with. Most of them now married and moved away. Some to America, to Australia, to Israel. So we dispersed, but we hope to meet next year when it will be 50 years since our arrival in England. This is from the same time, 1946, on the boat. You can see that we are on the boat. And it just shows you that our group varied from very small children-- I was one of the oldest, and therefore I felt responsible. And I was capable to help with the younger children, because we didn't have any nurses or otherwise, and they need to be helped. So this was taken inside the boat with some of the members of the group that we arrived in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm on the left. [LAUGHS] This is a photograph of Martin, my older son, who was born in 1949, who was killed in 1984. And it is the last photograph I have of him. I thought that this will be the end of me at that period. But it's amazing what you can go through, how much you can take in life. And he was a very exceptional person and a son. And he was just a very special person. Obviously, being a mother, I see swans in my ducklings, but he was something special. And losing him was a bit too much, at that time. But you have to continue somehow and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to accept whatever life dishes you out.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] around and their chanukiahs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is one of the family photographs which I have from the period when Martin was with us. And here on the picture we have Martin and Frances, which are at the back; and Nicole and Stephen. Nicole is on the left, Stephen is on the right. This is my younger son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I am in the middle holding a baby-- I think Rebecca-- or is it Jonathan, maybe? I don't recognize. It's too far. And there's two of my grandchildren, Daniel and David.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to meet on holidays, Pesach and Chanukah. They always came-- make an effort with Martin to come down from Glasgow to be with us. In a way, he wanted to-- to tell me, I'm with you. You know, he appreciate-- every Friday, he phoned. But also major Jewish holiday, he would come and join us. Frances had a very difficult time to be left with two small children-- Rebecca was four, Jonathan two-- and to bring them up on her own was a very difficult task to do. And a lot of it, I understand now, which perhaps I didn't understand as well at that time, because I was so preoccupied with my own pain, that perhaps I didn't understand quite how difficult it must have been to her. And somehow I didn't manage to communicate to her, which I feel-- I understand a lot. And there is-- I'm sad about the misunderstanding which arose from-- mainly from the religious point of view. I never wanted to influence or to dictate or to impose. I just felt that my grandchildren should be part of that culture. Perhaps not complete, but to know something, because I know Martin would have liked it. And I'm sure that this-- you can live in two cultures without any difficulty. One doesn't preclude the other. The result is that I have-- the grandchildren from Stephen live in three cultures. They've got a French mother, and therefore they speak at home French, and they're part of French culture. They are brought up in England, and they are English. And they are very proud being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it can be done. It's just that perhaps it was too difficult to cope with that. And it's one of my sadness, that I didn't manage to explain or to communicate or to bridge the gap. I failed. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the holidays when the children were together. Also, they don't see each other often. When they come together, there is a lovely family feeling about them. And I find it very encouraging. And this is Rebecca, on the right. Next to Rebecca is Jonathan. These are Martin's children. Then is Daniel and David, behind. And obviously, I always miss somebody out. This-- I don't have Joseph here. But maybe I'll find a photograph with Joseph, too. This is my next generation, which are growing very happy and developing in a very nice way. I'm very proud of them all. This is another photograph of my family. But, as usual, somebody is missing. So this time Rebecca is missing. Rebecca went to the garden to-- and she missed it. And she looked so beautiful in that day, and I'm sorry that I didn't manage to have her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we've got-- this was in Oxford, in Yarnton Manor, when we had the first of Dr. Martin Goldman lectures-- memorial lectures-- which I installed. And the theme of the lecture is the History of Polish-- connected with Polish Jews-- Jewry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there you have, on the left, Daniel. Next to Daniel is Joseph, the youngest. Then is David. And on the right is Jonathan, who came down with the family from Glasgow, which was lovely to have them all together. I just wish I would have made-- but I was so nervous, because I made a speech. And I was so tense and nervous that I didn't see the photographer. I should have managed that better. Well, this is my son Stephen. I'm very proud of him. He lives not far. And both Nicole and Stephen and the family are a great source of pleasure, support, and comfort for me. This was a special occasion. Again, the same location of the lecture, the memorial lecture, which I am on the left. And Professor Alexander, who at that time was head of Yarnton Manor, with us on this-- which happened-- it will be a lecture taking place every two years. This was in 1974. So the next-- '94. So the next lecture will be '96. In memory of Martin. This-- we left the best part for the end. This is the photograph of my husband, who died in 1972. He was also a survivor-- not a Holocaust survivor. He lost all his family in Warsaw. And he had a very interesting survival story to say. But he came-- I met him here in London. And he was a superb father. My children had the best father in the world. He had patience and time. And he loved the company of children. He opened the doors for them. I used to work on Saturday. He used to take them out, show them around. He was a wonderful, wonderful father to my two-- to Daniel-- to Stephen and Martin. He died young. He was 54.
